ideas.This sex which is not one:
"**This sex which is not one**" is the title of one of Luce Irigaray's most important works and the name of one of her most influential and provocative ideas — a rich, multi-layered phrase that encapsulates her radical rethinking of **female sexuality**, **sexual difference**, and women's exclusion from a masculine philosophical and cultural order. The phrase deliberately carries several interwoven meanings.

At one level, "this sex which is **not one**" means that female sexuality has been rendered **invisible and unrepresented** — that woman is "not one," not counted, not recognized as a genuine sex or subject in her own right within a culture and philosophy organized entirely around the *masculine* as the norm and the standard. In this "**phallocentric**" order, Irigaray argues, there is really only *one* sex that counts — the male — and woman is defined merely negatively, as man's opposite, complement, mirror, or lack (as in the Freudian view of female sexuality as defined by "penis envy" and absence). Woman does not exist as a positive sex of her own.

At another level, the phrase affirms that female sexuality and pleasure are "**not one**" in the sense of not being **singular, unified, or centralized** the way male sexuality (organized around the single phallus) is imagined to be. Irigaray famously contrasts the singular, visible, unitary phallic model with a female sexuality she describes as **plural, diffuse, multiple, and non-unified** — evoked through the image of the "**two lips**" (of the female genitals) that are always touching, in constant self-contact, and irreducible to a single organ or a unitary form. Female pleasure and identity, she suggests, are multiple, fluid, and plural rather than one. The phrase thus at once names woman's exclusion from a masculine "economy of the same" *and* affirms a positive, irreducible, multiple feminine specificity that resists reduction to the masculine model of singular identity. It is a central expression of Irigaray's project of thinking a genuine feminine subjectivity and sexual difference.
ideas.Sexual difference as the philosophical q:
The claim that **sexual difference** is "the philosophical question of our age" (or "of our time") is one of Luce Irigaray's most important and characteristic assertions — her conviction that the question of the difference between the sexes is not a marginal or merely social issue but *the* fundamental philosophical and ethical problem that our era must think through, and one that has been systematically **repressed and unthought** throughout the history of Western philosophy. This claim frames her entire project.

Irigaray famously declares (opening *An Ethics of Sexual Difference*) that "sexual difference is one of the major philosophical issues, if not *the* issue, of our age." Her point is that Western thought has *never genuinely thought* sexual difference — never conceived woman as a *positive*, autonomous other, a genuine second sex and subject with her own specificity. Instead, the entire philosophical tradition has been built on the *repression* of real sexual difference: it has taken the **masculine as the universal norm** and the standard of the human, and has understood "woman" only *in relation to* man — as his opposite, complement, mirror, negation, or lack — never as a subject in her own right. There has been, in effect, only *one* sex (the male) in the history of thought, with woman as its shadow. Genuine, irreducible sexual **difference** — two sexes, each with its own positive identity and mode of being — has remained unthought.

For Irigaray, thinking sexual difference authentically is therefore an enormous and urgent task with the deepest philosophical, ethical, and even ontological stakes. It would require nothing less than a transformation of our concepts of subjectivity, language, ethics, and being — creating the conditions for a genuine **feminine subjectivity** and a real relation and ethics *between* two different sexes, rather than the reduction of the feminine to the masculine "economy of the same." She envisions this as the possibility of a new "culture of sexual difference," a new ethics grounded in the recognition and cultivation of two irreducibly different subjects. This elevation of sexual difference to *the* central philosophical question of the age is foundational to Irigaray's thought and to a major current of feminist philosophy.
ideas.Critique of phallogocentrism:
Luce Irigaray's **critique of phallogocentrism** is the central critical project of her philosophy — her sustained, radical demonstration that the entire tradition of Western **philosophy, language, and culture** is structured by a masculine bias so deep and pervasive that it has systematically **excluded, erased, and silenced the feminine**. The term "**phallogocentrism**" (combining "phallocentrism" — centered on the phallus/masculine — with "logocentrism" — centered on the *logos*, reason, speech, presence) names this fusion of masculine dominance with the founding structures of Western reason itself.

Irigaray argues that Western thought, from Plato and Aristotle through Freud and Lacan, is not the neutral, universal, sexually-indifferent enterprise it claims to be. Rather, it is fundamentally **masculine** — organized around masculine subjectivity, masculine values, and a masculine "economy of the **same**," in which the male is taken as the norm and standard of the human, and everything is understood in terms of unity, identity, presence, and the singular (associated with the phallus). Within this order, the **feminine** has no place of its own: woman is represented only as man's *other* — his mirror, complement, negation, or lack — never as a positive subject in her own right. She functions as the silent ground, the material, the "mirror" that reflects man back to himself, while being denied her own voice, subjectivity, and mode of being.

Irigaray pursues this critique through close, subversive readings of the canonical philosophers (in *Speculum of the Other Woman*, she reads Freud, Plato, and others "against the grain," exposing the exclusion of the feminine at the founding of their systems). She argues that even **language** itself is structured phallogocentrically, offering no genuine means for women to express a feminine subjectivity. But her project is not merely negative: alongside the critique, Irigaray seeks to open space for a *positive* feminine — a different language ("*parler femme*," speaking as a woman), a different subjectivity, and a genuine **sexual difference** — that would escape the masculine economy of the same. This searching exposure of the masculine bias at the very foundations of Western reason and culture is Irigaray's most influential contribution to feminist philosophy.

works.Speculum of the Other Woman:
*Speculum of the Other Woman* (*Speculum de l'autre femme*, 1974) is Luce Irigaray's most important and influential work — a dense, difficult, and radically original book of feminist philosophy that mounts a devastating critique of the masculine bias at the foundations of Western **philosophy** and **psychoanalysis**, and that famously cost Irigaray her position (she was expelled from Lacan's school and lost her teaching post upon its publication). It is a founding text of French feminist theory.

The book's very title is a multi-layered provocation. A "**speculum**" is the concave medical instrument used to examine the interior of the female body — an image Irigaray turns into a figure for a different kind of reflection: not the *flat mirror* that reflects the masculine subject back to himself (the mirror of Western self-reflection), but a curved instrument that might allow the feminine, hitherto only the silent surface reflecting man, to be seen and to reflect *otherwise*. The "**other woman**" points to woman as the excluded, unrepresented other of masculine discourse.

Irigaray's method is a series of subversive, "**against the grain**" close readings of the canonical texts of Western thought, exposing how each is structured by the exclusion and erasure of the feminine. The book has a striking, deliberately non-linear structure: it opens with a critique of **Freud's** account of femininity (exposing how psychoanalysis defines woman only negatively, in terms of lack and "penis envy," as a defective man), moves through a central section engaging a range of philosophers, and culminates in a reading of **Plato's** allegory of the cave — locating the exclusion of the feminine (the maternal, the womb) at the very origin of Western metaphysics. Throughout, Irigaray demonstrates that the Western tradition is a "**phallogocentric**" order, an "economy of the same" in which woman functions only as man's mirror and other, never as a subject in her own right. Radical, allusive, and profoundly influential, *Speculum of the Other Woman* is a landmark of feminist philosophy and Irigaray's most important critique of the masculine foundations of Western thought.
works.This Sex Which Is Not One:
*This Sex Which Is Not One* (*Ce sexe qui n'en est pas un*, 1977) is one of Luce Irigaray's most important and accessible works — a collection of essays and interviews that develops the themes of her critique of masculine philosophy and psychoanalysis begun in *Speculum of the Other Woman*, and that articulates, in more direct form, her radical rethinking of **female sexuality**, subjectivity, and **sexual difference**. It is one of the founding texts of French feminism and a key statement of Irigaray's thought.

The title essay and the book as a whole elaborate the multi-layered idea captured in the phrase "**this sex which is not one**." On one hand, Irigaray argues that female sexuality has been rendered **invisible and unrepresentable** within a **phallocentric** order that recognizes only *one* sex — the masculine — and defines woman merely as man's negative, complement, or lack. Woman is "not one" — not counted, not a subject. On the other hand, she affirms that female sexuality and pleasure are "**not one**" in the sense of being irreducibly **plural, multiple, and non-unified** — not organized around a single organ or a unitary model (as male sexuality is imagined to be around the phallus). She famously evokes the image of the "**two lips**" in constant self-contact to figure a feminine sexuality that is diffuse, multiple, fluid, and irreducible to the singular, visible, phallic model.

The book also develops Irigaray's critique of the "**economy of the same**" that governs Western culture and exchange (she offers a provocative analysis, drawing on Marx and Lévi-Strauss, of women's position as *objects of exchange* between men), her call for women to develop their own language and voice ("*parler femme*," speaking-as-woman), and her vision of a genuine feminine subjectivity and a culture of **sexual difference** that would escape the masculine order. Combining critique with the positive project of thinking a feminine specificity, *This Sex Which Is Not One* is a central and influential statement of Irigaray's feminist philosophy of sexual difference.
